text: Every time you switch tasks, your brain pays a toll. Psychologist David Meyer calls this the "task-switch cost" — the measurable drop in speed and accuracy that follows any interruption. The cost arises because the prefrontal cortex must disengage one set of task-relevant rules and load another, a process that takes between 200 milliseconds and several seconds depending on task complexity. Meyer's studies found that even brief mental blocks created by task-switching can cost as much as 40 percent of productive time. The brain is not a computer that context-switches cleanly; each transition leaves residue.
question: According to David Meyer's research, how much productive time can be lost to task-switching?
